name: Halide
summary: Halide's representation explicitly separates the algorithm (what to compute) from the schedule (how to compute it: tile size, vectorization, parallelism, storage layout). The same algorithm can be re-scheduled without touching the algorithm code, exploring an enormous optimization search space.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Halide's representation explicitly separates the *algorithm* (what to compute) from the *schedule* (how to compute it: tile size, vectorization, parallelism, storage layout). The same algorithm can be re-scheduled without touching the algorithm code, exploring an enormous optimization search space.

sections.title: Algorithm/schedule split as IR design
sections.content:
This algorithm/schedule split is a distinctive IR design: most compilers embed scheduling decisions within the IR (loop nests, pragmas, layout choices), and changing them requires rewriting the program. Halide's separation lets auto-tuners (Halide's autoscheduler, Adams et al.) explore schedules automatically while the algorithm remains stable.

The pattern transfers to TVM TIR, IREE, and other tensor-graph compilers — the algorithm/schedule separation is the fundamental design idea that lets autotuners search over hardware-specific implementations of the same logical computation.
